<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`

```text
1	# Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1 — managed by chezmoi (Windows dotfiles).
2	#
3	# Thin loader: dot-sources every fragment in ~/.config/powershell/profile.d in
4	# sorted order, then the user's untracked local override. Keep logic in the
5	# fragments, not here — this file is intentionally boring.
6	
7	$ConfigRoot  = Join-Path $HOME '.config/powershell'
8	$FragmentDir = Join-Path $ConfigRoot 'profile.d'
9	$ModuleDir   = Join-Path $ConfigRoot 'modules'
10	
11	# Make chezmoi-managed modules importable (e.g. Copilot).
12	if (Test-Path $ModuleDir) {
13	    if (($env:PSModulePath -split [System.IO.Path]::PathSeparator) -notcontains $ModuleDir) {
14	        $env:PSModulePath = $ModuleDir + [System.IO.Path]::PathSeparator + $env:PSModulePath
15	    }
16	}
17	
18	if (Test-Path $FragmentDir) {
19	    foreach ($fragment in Get-ChildItem -Path $FragmentDir -Filter '*.ps1' | Sort-Object Name) {
20	        try { . $fragment.FullName }
21	        catch { Write-Warning "profile.d: failed to load $($fragment.Name): $_" }
22	    }
23	}
24	
25	# Untracked, user-local overrides (secrets, machine-specific tweaks).
26	$LocalProfile = Join-Path $ConfigRoot 'local.ps1'
27	if (Test-Path $LocalProfile) { . $LocalProfile }
28
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-10 16:42:27Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_02_profile_redirect.ps1`

```text
1	#Requires -Version 7
2	# run_onchange_after_02_profile_redirect.ps1 — make sure pwsh actually loads the
3	# managed $PROFILE.
4	#
5	# chezmoi writes the loader to the LITERAL ~/Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.
6	# PowerShell_profile.ps1. But when OneDrive "Known Folder Move" is enabled (very
7	# common on Windows), pwsh resolves $PROFILE under ~/OneDrive/Documents/
8	# PowerShell/... instead — so the managed profile silently never loads: no
9	# starship, no aliases, just the bare `PS C:\...>` prompt.
10	#
11	# We can't know that redirected path at apply time, but THIS script runs under
12	# pwsh, so $PROFILE here is exactly what an interactive pwsh computes. If it
13	# differs from the managed file, drop a one-line stub there that dot-sources the
14	# managed loader. Pure no-op when Documents isn't redirected.
15	#
16	# Fault-tolerant per the repo invariant: never abort the apply.
17	$ErrorActionPreference = 'Continue'
18	
19	$managed = Join-Path $HOME 'Documents\PowerShell\Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1'
20	$real    = $PROFILE   # CurrentUserCurrentHost — OneDrive-aware
21	
22	try {
23	    $managedFull = [System.IO.Path]::GetFullPath($managed)
24	    $realFull    = if ($real) { [System.IO.Path]::GetFullPath([string]$real) } else { '' }
25	
26	    if ($realFull -and ($realFull -ine $managedFull)) {
27	        $dir = Split-Path $realFull -Parent
28	        if (-not (Test-Path $dir)) { New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $dir | Out-Null }
29	
30	        # Preserve any PRE-EXISTING real profile before we overwrite it — the
31	        # run_once_before backup only guards the literal ~/Documents path, not
32	        # this OneDrive-redirected one. Skip if it's already our own stub.
33	        $marker = 'Auto-written by chezmoi (dotfiles-windows)'
34	        if (Test-Path -LiteralPath $realFull) {
35	            $existing = Get-Content -LiteralPath $realFull -Raw -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
36	            if ($existing -and ($existing -notmatch [regex]::Escape($marker))) {
37	                $backupDir = Join-Path $HOME '.dotfiles-backup'
38	                New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $backupDir | Out-Null
39	                $bak = Join-Path $backupDir 'Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1.onedrive.bak'
40	                if (-not (Test-Path -LiteralPath $bak)) {
41	                    Copy-Item -LiteralPath $realFull -Destination $bak -Force
42	                    Write-Host "==> backed up existing profile -> $bak" -ForegroundColor Cyan
43	                }
44	            }
45	        }
46	
47	        $stub = "# Auto-written by chezmoi (dotfiles-windows). Your Documents folder is`n" +
48	                "# OneDrive-redirected, so pwsh looks here instead of ~/Documents. Load the`n" +
49	                "# real managed profile:`n" +
50	                ". `"$managedFull`"`n"
51	        Set-Content -LiteralPath $realFull -Value $stub -Encoding utf8
52	        Write-Host "==> Wrote profile redirect stub -> $realFull" -ForegroundColor Cyan
53	    } else {
54	        Write-Host '==> $PROFILE points at the managed loader (no redirect needed)' -ForegroundColor DarkGray
55	    }
56	} catch {
57	    Write-Warning "profile redirect stub failed (non-fatal): $_"
58	}
59
```

 `./.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_02_profile_redirect.ps1`

```text
1	#Requires -Version 7
2	# run_onchange_after_02_profile_redirect.ps1 — make sure pwsh actually loads the
3	# managed $PROFILE.
4	#
5	# chezmoi writes the loader to the LITERAL ~/Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.
6	# PowerShell_profile.ps1. But when OneDrive "Known Folder Move" is enabled (very
7	# common on Windows), pwsh resolves $PROFILE under ~/OneDrive/Documents/
8	# PowerShell/... instead — so the managed profile silently never loads: no
9	# starship, no aliases, just the bare `PS C:\...>` prompt.
10	#
11	# We can't know that redirected path at apply time, but THIS script runs under
12	# pwsh, so $PROFILE here is exactly what an interactive pwsh computes. If it
13	# differs from the managed file, drop a one-line stub there that dot-sources the
14	# managed loader. Pure no-op when Documents isn't redirected.
15	#
16	# Fault-tolerant per the repo invariant: never abort the apply.
17	$ErrorActionPreference = 'Continue'
18	
19	$managed = Join-Path $HOME 'Documents\PowerShell\Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1'
20	$real    = $PROFILE   # CurrentUserCurrentHost — OneDrive-aware
21	
22	try {
23	    $managedFull = [System.IO.Path]::GetFullPath($managed)
24	    $realFull    = if ($real) { [System.IO.Path]::GetFullPath([string]$real) } else { '' }
25	
26	    if ($realFull -and ($realFull -ine $managedFull)) {
27	        $dir = Split-Path $realFull -Parent
28	        if (-not (Test-Path $dir)) { New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $dir | Out-Null }
29	
30	        # Preserve any PRE-EXISTING real profile before we overwrite it — the
31	        # run_once_before backup only guards the literal ~/Documents path, not
32	        # this OneDrive-redirected one. Skip if it's already our own stub.
33	        $marker = 'Auto-written by chezmoi (dotfiles-windows)'
34	        if (Test-Path -LiteralPath $realFull) {
35	            $existing = Get-Content -LiteralPath $realFull -Raw -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
36	            if ($existing -and ($existing -notmatch [regex]::Escape($marker))) {
37	                $backupDir = Join-Path $HOME '.dotfiles-backup'
38	                New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $backupDir | Out-Null
39	                $bak = Join-Path $backupDir 'Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1.onedrive.bak'
40	                if (-not (Test-Path -LiteralPath $bak)) {
41	                    Copy-Item -LiteralPath $realFull -Destination $bak -Force
42	                    Write-Host "==> backed up existing profile -> $bak" -ForegroundColor Cyan
43	                }
44	            }
45	        }
46	
47	        $stub = "# Auto-written by chezmoi (dotfiles-windows). Your Documents folder is`n" +
48	                "# OneDrive-redirected, so pwsh looks here instead of ~/Documents. Load the`n" +
49	                "# real managed profile:`n" +
50	                ". `"$managedFull`"`n"
51	        Set-Content -LiteralPath $realFull -Value $stub -Encoding utf8
52	        Write-Host "==> Wrote profile redirect stub -> $realFull" -ForegroundColor Cyan
53	    } else {
54	        Write-Host '==> $PROFILE points at the managed loader (no redirect needed)' -ForegroundColor DarkGray
55	    }
56	} catch {
57	    Write-Warning "profile redirect stub failed (non-fatal): $_"
58	}
59
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_20_editor_overlays.ps1.tmpl`

```text
1	#Requires -Version 7
2	# run_onchange_after_20_editor_overlays.ps1 — merge a shared settings overlay
3	# into VSCode + Cursor, and seed keybindings once. Re-runs when either embedded
4	# source file changes. Skips an editor whose User dir doesn't exist (not installed).
5	
6	$ErrorActionPreference = 'Continue'   # best-effort; never abort the apply
7	
8	# Embedded at render time from the (chezmoi-ignored) editors/ sources — so this
9	# script is self-contained and re-runs when they change.
10	$overlayJson = @'
11	{{ include "editors/vscode-overlay.json" }}
12	'@
13	$keybindingsJson = @'
14	{{ include "editors/keybindings.json" }}
15	'@
16	
17	$overlay = $overlayJson | ConvertFrom-Json -AsHashtable
18	
19	function Merge-EditorSettings([string]$userDir) {
20	    if (-not (Test-Path $userDir)) { return }   # editor not installed
21	    $settingsPath = Join-Path $userDir 'settings.json'
22	    $settings = @{}
23	    if (Test-Path $settingsPath) {
24	        $raw = Get-Content -Raw $settingsPath
25	        # best-effort JSONC: drop whole-line // comments and /* */ blocks.
26	        $raw = [regex]::Replace($raw, '(?m)^\s*//.*$', '')
27	        $raw = [regex]::Replace($raw, '/\*.*?\*/', '', 'Singleline')
28	        if ($raw.Trim()) { try { $settings = $raw | ConvertFrom-Json -AsHashtable } catch { Write-Warning "could not parse $settingsPath; overlaying onto empty"; $settings = @{} } }
29	    }
30	    foreach ($k in $overlay.Keys) { $settings[$k] = $overlay[$k] }
31	    New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $userDir | Out-Null
32	    $settings | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 12 | Set-Content -Path $settingsPath -Encoding utf8
33	    Write-Host "editor overlay -> $settingsPath"
34	
35	    # Seed keybindings once; never clobber the user's edits.
36	    $kbPath = Join-Path $userDir 'keybindings.json'
37	    if (-not (Test-Path $kbPath)) {
38	        $keybindingsJson | Set-Content -Path $kbPath -Encoding utf8
39	        Write-Host "seeded keybindings -> $kbPath"
40	    }
41	}
42	
43	foreach ($editor in 'Code', 'Cursor') {
44	    Merge-EditorSettings (Join-Path $env:APPDATA "$editor\User")
45	}
46
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./.chezmoiscripts/run_once_before_01_backup.ps1.tmpl`

```text
1	#Requires -Version 7
2	# run_once_before_01_backup.ps1 — snapshot pre-existing config before the first
3	# apply overwrites it. backupMode: {{ .backupMode }}
4	#   off        skip entirely
5	#   smart/full back up the managed targets that already exist (on Windows the two
6	#              modes are equivalent — there is no broader allowlist)
7	{{ if ne .backupMode "off" -}}
8	$ErrorActionPreference = 'Continue'   # best-effort; never block the apply
9	
10	$targets = @(
11	    (Join-Path $HOME 'Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1'),
12	    (Join-Path $HOME '.config/powershell'),
13	    (Join-Path $HOME '.config/starship.toml'),
14	    (Join-Path $env:APPDATA 'alacritty/alacritty.toml'),
15	    (Join-Path $env:APPDATA 'Code/User/settings.json'),
16	    (Join-Path $env:APPDATA 'Cursor/User/settings.json')
17	)
18	$existing = $targets | Where-Object { Test-Path $_ }
19	if ($existing) {
20	    $stamp = Get-Date -Format 'yyyyMMdd-HHmmss'
21	    $dir = Join-Path $HOME ".dotfiles-backup/$stamp"
22	    New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $dir | Out-Null
23	    foreach ($t in $existing) {
24	        Copy-Item -Recurse -Force -LiteralPath $t -Destination (Join-Path $dir (Split-Path $t -Leaf))
25	    }
26	    Write-Host "backed up $($existing.Count) item(s) -> $dir"
27	}
28	{{ end -}}
29
```

Show windows workflow yml or find workflow files

```text
name: windows

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: windows-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install chezmoi + PowerShell modules
        shell: pwsh
        run: |
          $ok = $false
          foreach ($try in 1..3) {
            winget install --id twpayne.chezmoi -e --silent --accept-source-agreements --accept-package-agreements
            if ($LASTEXITCODE -eq 0) { $ok = $true; break }
            Write-Host "winget chezmoi attempt $try failed (exit $LASTEXITCODE); retrying..."
            Start-Sleep 8
          }
          echo "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\WinGet\Links" >> $env:GITHUB_PATH
          if (-not $ok) {
            Write-Host 'winget failed after retries; falling back to scoop'
            irm get.scoop.sh -OutFile "$env:TEMP\scoop.ps1"
            & "$env:TEMP\scoop.ps1" -RunAsAdmin
            scoop install chezmoi
            echo "$HOME\scoop\shims" >> $env:GITHUB_PATH
          }
          Install-Module PSScriptAnalyzer -Force -Scope CurrentUser
          Install-Module Pester -Force -Scope CurrentUser -SkipPublisherCheck
          $global:LASTEXITCODE = 0

      - name: Lint PowerShell (PSScriptAnalyzer)
        shell: pwsh
        run: |
          $issues = Invoke-ScriptAnalyzer -Path . -Recurse -Settings ./PSScriptAnalyzerSettings.psd1
          $issues | Format-Table -AutoSize
          if ($issues | Where-Object Severity -eq 'Error') { throw "PSScriptAnalyzer found $(($issues | Where-Object Severity -eq 'Error').Count) error(s)" }

      - name: chezmoi init (writes config from prompt answers)
        shell: pwsh
        run: |
          $flags = @(
            '--promptChoice','Role: workstation (full desktop) or minimal (shell only)=minimal',
            '--promptString','Your full name (git)=CI Runner',
            '--promptString','Your git email=ci@example.com',
            '--promptBool','Install coding agents (Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex, Copilot CLI, SpecStory)=false',
            '--promptBool','Install GUI apps via winget (VSCode, Cursor, Notepad++, Terminal, Alacritty, PowerToys, Raycast)=false',
            '--promptBool','Install utility apps via winget (CPU-Z, GPU-Z, TreeSize, VLC, Everything, ShareX, HWiNFO)=false',
            '--promptBool','Install gaming apps (Steam)=false',
            '--promptBool','Install extra runtimes (rust, go, ruby) beyond node/bun/uv=false',
            '--promptBool','Install media CLIs (ffmpeg, imagemagick)=false',
            '--promptBool','Install local LLM tools (Ollama, LiteLLM)=false',
            '--promptBool','Install tunnel tools (ngrok, cloudflared)=false',
            '--promptBool','Install Infrastructure-as-Code tools (Azure CLI, Terraform, OpenTofu)=false',
            '--promptBool','Use China (GFW) mirrors for pip/npm/cargo/go/node=false',
            '--promptBool','Managed or corporate machine (skip org-policy-blocked apps like Tailscale and Grammarly)=false',
            '--promptChoice','Back up existing files before apply (smart|full|off)=off',
            '--promptBool','Enable PSReadLine vi editing mode=true'
          )
          chezmoi init --source $PWD --no-tty @flags

      - name: Parse-check rendered .ps1.tmpl scripts
        shell: pwsh
        run: |
          $failed = $false
          Get-ChildItem -Recurse -Filter *.ps1.tmpl | ForEach-Object {
            $rendered = Get-Content -Raw $_.FullName | chezmoi execute-template --source .
            if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0 -or -not $rendered) { Write-Host "RENDER FAIL: $($_.Name)"; $failed = $true; return }
            $errors = $null
            [System.Management.Automation.Language.Parser]::ParseInput($rendered, [ref]$null, [ref]$errors) | Out-Null
            if ($errors) { Write-Host "PARSE FAIL: $($_.Name)"; $errors | ForEach-Object { Write-Host "  $_" }; $failed = $true }
            else { Write-Host "OK:   $($_.Name)" }
          }
          if ($failed) { throw 'A rendered template failed to render or parse' }

      - name: chezmoi apply (files only, no package scripts)
        shell: pwsh
        run: |
          $dest = Join-Path $env:RUNNER_TEMP 'dothome'
          New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $dest | Out-Null
          chezmoi apply --source $PWD --destination $dest --exclude scripts --verbose
          Write-Host '--- deployed tree ---'
          Get-ChildItem -Recurse -File $dest | ForEach-Object { $_.FullName.Replace($dest,'') }

      - name: Pester tests
        shell: pwsh
        run: |
          if (Get-ChildItem -Recurse -Filter *.Tests.ps1) {
            Invoke-Pester -CI -Path . -Output Detailed
          } else { Write-Host 'no Pester tests yet' }
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`

```text
1	# Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1 — managed by chezmoi (Windows dotfiles).
2	#
3	# Thin loader: dot-sources every fragment in ~/.config/powershell/profile.d in
4	# sorted order, then the user's untracked local override. Keep logic in the
5	# fragments, not here — this file is intentionally boring.
6	
7	$ConfigRoot  = Join-Path $HOME '.config/powershell'
8	$FragmentDir = Join-Path $ConfigRoot 'profile.d'
9	$ModuleDir   = Join-Path $ConfigRoot 'modules'
10	
11	# Make chezmoi-managed modules importable (e.g. Copilot).
12	if (Test-Path $ModuleDir) {
13	    if (($env:PSModulePath -split [System.IO.Path]::PathSeparator) -notcontains $ModuleDir) {
14	        $env:PSModulePath = $ModuleDir + [System.IO.Path]::PathSeparator + $env:PSModulePath
15	    }
16	}
17	
18	if (Test-Path $FragmentDir) {
19	    foreach ($fragment in Get-ChildItem -Path $FragmentDir -Filter '*.ps1' | Sort-Object Name) {
20	        try { . $fragment.FullName }
21	        catch { Write-Warning "profile.d: failed to load $($fragment.Name): $_" }
22	    }
23	}
24	
25	# Untracked, user-local overrides (secrets, machine-specific tweaks).
26	$LocalProfile = Join-Path $ConfigRoot 'local.ps1'
27	if (Test-Path $LocalProfile) { . $LocalProfile }
28
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
1	---
2	name: dotfiles-windows
3	description: Operate and navigate THIS Windows machine's chezmoi-managed dotfiles (native PowerShell 7). Use when the user asks how to apply/edit/diff dotfiles, where a config or tool lives, how the pwsh $PROFILE / copilot-proxy / scoop+winget install works, how a keybinding or helper (applaunch/sysvol/x/cas) works, or how to use chezmoi / just in this repo.
4	---
5	
6	# dotfiles-windows
7	
8	This machine's `$HOME` is managed by a **chezmoi** dotfiles repo targeting **native
9	Windows + PowerShell 7**. Packages install via **scoop** (CLI) + **winget** (GUI) —
10	there is no ansible here. Repo: <https://github.com/daviddwlee84/dotfiles-windows>.
11	(macOS/Linux/WSL are handled by the separate `daviddwlee84/dotfiles` repo.)
12	
13	## Orientation
14	- Source dir: `chezmoi source-path` — edit there (`cd "$(chezmoi source-path)"`).
15	- Preview / apply: `chezmoi diff` / `chezmoi apply` (or `just diff` / `just apply`).
16	- Source of a target: `chezmoi source-path <path>`; render without applying: `chezmoi cat <path>`.
17	- Re-run init prompts: `chezmoi init` (answers persist in `~/.config/chezmoi/chezmoi.toml`).
18	- `docs/**`, `editors/**`, `scripts/**` are chezmoi-ignored (not deployed).
19	
20	## Shell layout (pwsh)
21	- `$PROFILE` (`~/Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`) is a loader
22	  that dot-sources `~/.config/powershell/profile.d/*.ps1` in sorted order, then
23	  `~/.config/powershell/local.ps1` (untracked user override, loaded last).
24	- Fragments: `00_env` (PATH/XDG/env), `10_tools` (starship, zoxide, atuin,
25	  fzf, direnv, tv), `20_aliases` (`ll`/`gs`/`reload`/`cas`/`cau`/`chezmoi-cd`/`run-for`),
26	  `30_apps` (`applaunch`/`appquit`/`apprestart`/`sysvol`/`sysmute`/`x`), `35_yazi` (`y`),
27	  `40_copilot` (imports the Copilot module), `90_psreadline` (vi-mode gated on `enableVimMode`).
28	
29	## Packages
30	- `.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_10_packages.ps1.tmpl` is the **single source of
31	  truth** for installs, gated by the init toggles. scoop = CLI tools, winget = GUI
32	  apps, npm = AI agents, PSGallery = PSFzf/AudioDeviceCmdlets. Fault-tolerant: one
33	  failed package is collected + reported, never aborts the apply.
34	- Install ≠ upgrade: `chezmoi apply` only installs what's missing; upgrade via
35	  `just upgrade-scoop` / `just upgrade-winget`.
36	
37	## copilot-proxy
38	- Module at `~/.config/powershell/modules/Copilot`. Commands: `copilot-proxy`
39	  (`start`/`stop`/`restart`/`status`/`doctor`/`logs`/`shim`/`whoami`/`auth`),
40	  `copilot-run`, `claude-copilot`, `claude-copilot-once`, `copilot-here`,
41	  `copilot-model`, `copilot-embed`, `semsearch`.
42	- Needs `bun`. Token: `~/.local/share/copilot-api/github_token`. Ports 4141 (proxy) /
43	  4142 (throttle shim). Default model `claude-opus-4-8[1m]`. `copilot-here` writes
44	  only the gitignored `./.claude/settings.local.json`. Full guide: `docs/copilot-proxy.md`.
45	
46	## Editors / terminal / tv
47	- VSCode & Cursor settings + keybindings are deep-merged into `%APPDATA%\{Code,Cursor}\User`
48	  by `.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_20_editor_overlays.ps1.tmpl` (sources under `editors/`).
49	- Windows Terminal `profiles.defaults` and Alacritty (`%APPDATA%\alacritty\alacritty.toml`)
50	  are managed too. Television channels live under `%APPDATA%\television\cable\` (`tv <name>`).
51	
52	## What's enabled on THIS machine
53	- role: **{{ .role }}**
54	- Coding agents: {{ .installCodingAgents }} · GUI apps: {{ .installWindowsApps }} · Utility apps: {{ .installUtilityApps }} · Gaming: {{ .installGamingApps }}
55	- Extra runtimes: {{ .installExtraRuntimes }} · Media: {{ .installMediaTools }} · LLM: {{ .installLlmTools }} · Tunnel: {{ .installTunnelTools }} · IaC: {{ .installIacTools }}
56	- China mirrors: {{ .useChineseMirror }} · Backup mode: {{ .backupMode }} · Vim mode: {{ .enableVimMode }} · Managed machine: {{ .managedMachine }}
57	
58	## just recipes
59	`just --list`: `apply`/`diff`/`update`, `upgrade-scoop`/`upgrade-winget`, `lint`/`test`,
60	`docs-serve`/`docs-build`, and `docker-up`/`docker-down`/`docker-clean`/`docker-logs`
61	(the x86-Linux+KVM Windows-in-Docker test harness — see `docker/windows/`).
62	
63	## Gotchas
64	- Windows-only repo — no `{{ "{{" }} if eq .chezmoi.os {{ "}}" }}` branching needed.
65	- Editor settings use a `run_onchange` pwsh merger (not `modify_`) on Windows.
66	- tmux / zellij are Unix-only and intentionally absent; **WezTerm** (installed) is
67	  the native tmux-like multiplexer, or use Windows Terminal panes. Runtimes are
68	  native via scoop (node/bun/go/rust/ruby) + uv for Python — no mise on Windows.
69	- This skill body is shared: `dot_agents/skills/dotfiles-windows/` and
70	  `dot_claude/skills/dotfiles-windows/` both render `.chezmoitemplates/dotfiles-windows-skill.md`.
71
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./docs/shell.md`

```text
1	# Shell & environment
2	
3	How a PowerShell session is assembled — what loads, how to reload, and how
4	`PATH` and the XDG base dirs get set — **without ever touching the Windows
5	"Environment Variables" GUI**.
6	
7	## Profile loading
8	
9	`$PROFILE` (`~/Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`) is a thin
10	loader. It dot-sources every fragment under `~/.config/powershell/profile.d/*.ps1`
11	in sorted (numeric-prefix) order, then your untracked `local.ps1` last. All the
12	logic lives in the fragments; the loader stays boring.
13	
14	| Fragment | Does |
15	|---|---|
16	| `00_env.ps1` | XDG base dirs, `$env:EDITOR`, PATH |
17	| `05_mirrors.ps1` | China package mirrors (when enabled) |
18	| `10_tools.ps1` | `init` hooks: starship, zoxide, mise, atuin, fzf, direnv, tv |
19	| `20_aliases.ps1` | aliases + helpers (`reload`, `cas`/`cau`, git shortcuts) |
20	| `28_tldr.ps1` | `tldrf` — tldr with a `zh_TW → zh → en` language fallback |
21	| `30_apps.ps1` | app control (`applaunch`/`appquit`/…), audio, clipboard |
22	| `35_yazi.ps1` | `y` — launch yazi, cd to where you quit |
23	| `40_copilot.ps1` | import the `copilot-proxy` PowerShell module |
24	| `90_psreadline.ps1` | PSReadLine (vi mode, history) |
25	
26	Modules under `~/.config/powershell/modules` are prepended to
27	`$env:PSModulePath` by the loader, so they import by name (e.g. `Copilot`).
28	
29	## Reloading
30	
31	`reload` **dot-sources** the profile back into the current session — the same
32	mechanism `$PROFILE` uses at startup:
33	
34	```powershell
35	function reload { . $PROFILE }                    # re-source everything
36	function cas { chezmoi apply  @args; . $PROFILE } # apply, then reload
37	function cau { chezmoi update @args; . $PROFILE } # git pull + apply, then reload
38	```
39	
40	!!! note "reload is additive, not a clean restart"
41	    Dot-sourcing **re-runs** the profile in place. PATH edits are idempotent
42	    (guarded, never double-added) and `Set-Alias` / `Import-Module` just
43	    re-apply — but anything a *previous* load defined that this load no longer
44	    defines is **not** removed. For a guaranteed-clean state, open a new pwsh
45	    session.
46	
47	## PATH: Windows vs Unix
48	
49	On **Unix**, `PATH` is a single colon-separated variable the shell rc
50	(`.zshrc` / `.bashrc`) assembles at startup — purely process-scoped, inherited
51	by child processes, no system database involved. Edit a dotfile, and it changes
52	on the next shell.
53	
54	**Windows** has two *persistent* layers stored in the **registry**, plus the
55	in-process copy:
56	
57	| Layer | Where | Scope |
58	|---|---|---|
59	| Machine PATH | `HKLM\…\Session Manager\Environment` | all users (needs admin) |
60	| User PATH | `HKCU\Environment` | your account, all your processes |
61	| `$env:PATH` | in memory, per process | this session + its children |
62	
63	At logon Windows concatenates Machine + User into each process's `$env:PATH`.
64	Editing the registry does **not** update already-running processes.
65	
66	### We change PATH without the GUI
67	
68	This repo touches `PATH` two ways, both scripted — the System Properties
69	"Environment Variables" dialog is never opened:
70	
71	1. **Persistent (registry User PATH) — scoop only.** When scoop installs, it
72	   writes `~/scoop/shims` into the User PATH programmatically
73	   (`[Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable('Path', …, 'User')`), so scoop CLIs
74	   resolve in *every* shell and GUI app — no admin, no dialog.
75	
76	2. **In-process only — our profile.** `00_env.ps1` prepends `~/.local/bin` and
77	   `~/scoop/shims` to `$env:PATH` on every shell start, idempotently and only
78	   when the directory exists:
79	
80	   ```powershell
81	   $UserPaths = @(
82	       (Join-Path $HOME '.local/bin'),
83	       (Join-Path $HOME 'scoop/shims')
84	   ) | Where-Object { $_ -and (Test-Path $_) } | Select-Object -Unique
85	   foreach ($p in $UserPaths) {
86	       if (($env:PATH -split ';') -notcontains $p) { $env:PATH = "$p;$env:PATH" }
87	   }
88	   ```
89	
90	`bootstrap.ps1` does a third, one-shot variant: right after scoop installs the
91	baseline tools it rebuilds `$env:PATH` from the `Machine + User` registry values
92	so the just-installed shims resolve **in the same session** (scoop persisted
93	them, but the running process's copy predates that write).
94	
95	!!! warning "`~/.local/bin` is pwsh-session-scoped"
96	    `~/.local/bin` is the Windows home for your own scripts and binaries — same
97	    path name as Unix. But unlike `~/scoop/shims`, it is added to `$env:PATH`
98	    **only** by this profile; it is **never** persisted to the registry. So it's
99	    on PATH inside pwsh sessions that load the profile, but **not** in Windows
100	    PowerShell 5.1, in GUI apps, or in anything launched outside pwsh. To make a
101	    binary there visible everywhere, add `~/.local/bin` to the User PATH
102	    yourself (`setx` or `[Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable(…, 'User')`).
103	
104	Resolution order after the profile runs: `~/scoop/shims` → `~/.local/bin` →
105	inherited PATH (each is prepended, so the last one added ends up first).
106	
107	## XDG base dirs on Windows
108	
109	XDG and Windows split directories on **different axes** — XDG by *purpose*
110	(config / data / state / cache), Windows by *whether it roams* (Roaming vs
111	Local) — so there is no clean 1:1. The rough mapping:
112	
113	| XDG (Unix) | Closest Windows folder |
114	|---|---|
115	| `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` (`~/.config`) | `%APPDATA%` = `AppData\Roaming` |
116	| `XDG_DATA_HOME` (`~/.local/share`) | `%APPDATA%` = `AppData\Roaming` |
117	| `XDG_STATE_HOME` (`~/.local/state`) | `%LOCALAPPDATA%` = `AppData\Local` |
118	| `XDG_CACHE_HOME` (`~/.cache`) | `%LOCALAPPDATA%` = `AppData\Local` |
119	
120	`AppData\Roaming` bundles what XDG calls *config* and *data* into one folder;
121	`AppData\Local` bundles *cache*, *state*, and non-roaming data. ("Roaming" only
122	actually roams under domain Roaming Profiles / Enterprise State Roaming — on a
123	standalone PC it's just a folder with that name.)
124	
125	Rather than live with the ambiguity, `00_env.ps1` sets the XDG variables to
126	Unix-style paths, so **XDG-aware tools keep a tidy `$HOME` shared with the
127	macOS/Linux dotfiles**:
128	
129	```powershell
130	$env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME = Join-Path $HOME '.config'
131	$env:XDG_DATA_HOME   = Join-Path $HOME '.local/share'
132	$env:XDG_STATE_HOME  = Join-Path $HOME '.local/state'
133	$env:XDG_CACHE_HOME  = Join-Path $HOME '.cache'
134	$env:YAZI_CONFIG_HOME = Join-Path $env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME 'yazi'  # yazi defaults to %APPDATA%
135	```
136	
137	starship, atuin, zoxide, and yazi then read `~/.config`. Apps that **hard-code
138	`%APPDATA%`** and ignore XDG — VSCode, Cursor, Alacritty — stay in
139	`AppData\Roaming`, which is why the repo tracks `AppData/Roaming/alacritty/…`
140	and the backup script's allowlist names `%APPDATA%\Code`, `%APPDATA%\Cursor`,
141	and `%APPDATA%\alacritty`.
142	
143	So in this repo `AppData\Roaming` is **not** "Windows XDG" — it's simply where
144	the non-XDG native apps land. The real XDG role is played by the `~/.config`
145	paths we set explicitly.
146
```
</details></tool-use>

## Conventions & hard invariants

1. **A new/renamed init prompt must update the CI flags in the same commit.** `chezmoi init --promptBool/--promptChoice "<text>=value"` matches the prompt's exact TEXT (3rd arg to `promptXOnce` in `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl`), used non-interactively by `.github/workflows/windows.yml`. Forget it → CI's init hangs/EOFs. Corollary: **never put `=` in a prompt's text** — the `name=value` parser splits on the first `=` (this broke the `role` prompt once).
2. **Run-scripts must never abort the apply.** The installer + editor/terminal/backup/skill scripts use `$ErrorActionPreference='Continue'` (+ `PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference=$false`), collect failures via `Register-Failure`, print a summary, and exit 0. One bad scoop/winget manifest (e.g. btop's stale `.conf`) must not stop the rest.
3. **Preserve raw escapes when authoring config with control chars.** Alacritty CSI-u (`[…u`), VSCode `sendSequence`, tv `\t` — hand-typing tends to drop them. Generate via a throwaway Python script and re-parse (`tomllib` / `json`) to confirm the escape landed.
4. **tv channel commands are `pwsh -NoProfile -Command "…"` inside TOML literal (`'''…'''`) strings** — runs regardless of tv's shell, sidesteps nested-quote escaping; placeholders `{split: :0}` (space) / `{split:\t:0}` (tab). tv reads config from `%APPDATA%\television\` on Windows.
5. **Editors use a run_onchange pwsh merger, not `modify_`** (`modify_` interpreter selection is unreliable on Windows). Overlay sources live in `editors/` (chezmoi-ignored, embedded via `{{ include }}`).
6. **The agent skill body lives once** in `.chezmoitemplates/dotfiles-windows-skill.md`; both `dot_agents/skills/dotfiles-windows/SKILL.md.tmpl` and `dot_claude/skills/dotfiles-windows/SKILL.md.tmpl` render it via `{{ "{{" }} template … {{ "}}" }}` → real files at `~/.agents/skills/` and `~/.claude/skills/`. Edit the shared template, not the two one-line renderers (no symlink/junction — those need elevation/Developer Mode on Windows).
7. **PSScriptAnalyzer exclusions are intentional** (`PSScriptAnalyzerSettings.psd1`): hyphenated command names, `Write-Host`, `Invoke-Expression` for tool init, no-BOM UTF-8. Don't "fix" these; do fix real Errors.

### Cross-file mirrors (same commit)

- New installer tool → `docs/tools.md` **and** `docs/tools.zh-TW.md`.
- New init prompt → `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl` + `windows.yml` flags + `docs/setup.md`/`setup.zh-TW.md` tables + the "What's enabled" block in `.chezmoitemplates/dotfiles-windows-skill.md`.
- New `docs/**/*.md` → its `.zh-TW.md` twin + `nav` + `nav_translations` in `mkdocs.yml`; `just docs-build` must stay green.
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./backlog/align-windows-backup-with-unix-run-before-unified-dir.md`

```text
1	# Align Windows backup with Unix: run_before + unified dir
2	
3	**Status**: P?
4	**Effort**: S
5	**Related**: `TODO.md` · `.chezmoiscripts/run_once_before_01_backup.ps1.tmpl` · Unix repo `run_before_01_backup_dotfiles.sh.tmpl`
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	2026-07, surfaced while comparing the Windows `bootstrap.ps1` install path
10	against the Unix `get.chezmoi.io` one-liner. Both repos carry a pre-apply
11	backup run-script, but they are **not behaviorally equivalent**, and the
12	divergence means the Windows side offers weaker ongoing protection.
13	
14	## Investigation
15	
16	Two differences confirmed by reading both scripts:
17	
18	1. **Trigger frequency.**
19	   - Unix: `run_before_01_backup_dotfiles.sh.tmpl` — `run_before_` fires
20	     **before every `chezmoi apply`**, with a daily-dedup guard, and uses
21	     `chezmoi status` "smart" mode to back up only files whose col2 ∈ {M,D}
22	     (about to be overwritten/deleted). Ongoing protection on every apply.
23	   - Windows: `.chezmoiscripts/run_once_before_01_backup.ps1.tmpl` —
24	     `run_once_before_` fires **only on the first apply** (or when the script
25	     content hash changes). After the initial snapshot, editing a local file
26	     and re-applying does **not** back it up. This is the real gap.
27	
28	2. **Selection strategy.**
29	   - Unix: smart (status-driven) vs full (fixed allowlist) vs off.
30	   - Windows: fixed target allowlist only (smart == full); no status-driven
31	     selection. Allowlist currently:
32	     `Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`,
33	     `.config/powershell`, `.config/starship.toml`,
34	     `%APPDATA%/alacritty/alacritty.toml`,
35	     `%APPDATA%/{Code,Cursor}/User/settings.json`.
36	
37	3. **Backup dir naming inconsistency.**
38	   - Unix writes to `~/.dotfiles_backup` (underscore).
39	   - Windows writes to `~/.dotfiles-backup` (hyphen).
40	
41	## Options considered
42	
43	| Option | Pros | Cons |
44	|---|---|---|
45	| A. Rename `run_once_before_` → `run_before_` on Windows | Every-apply protection, matches Unix intent | `chezmoi status` parsing in PowerShell needed for true smart mode; more work per apply |
46	| B. Keep `run_once_`, just unify dir name | Trivial, low risk | Leaves the first-apply-only gap unaddressed |
47	| C. Full parity: `run_before_` + PowerShell `chezmoi status` smart-select + unified dir | Real cross-platform symmetry | Most effort; must parse status output & map target paths on Windows |
48	
49	## Current blocker / open questions
50	
51	- Is every-apply backup actually wanted on Windows, or is first-apply-only an
52	  intentional lighter-touch choice? Need user preference before picking A/C.
53	- If going smart: does `chezmoi status` output on Windows map cleanly from
54	  relpath → on-disk target for the parse loop (APPDATA-rooted targets aren't
55	  under `$HOME`)?
56	- Dir rename: pick one name for both repos (`~/.dotfiles-backup` reads better;
57	  Unix uses underscore) — renaming Unix side touches the other repo.
58	
59	## Decision (if any)
60	
61	None yet — logged for later. Lowest-risk slice is Option B (dir-name unify);
62	full value is Option C.
63	
64	## References
65	
66	- Windows backup: `.chezmoiscripts/run_once_before_01_backup.ps1.tmpl`
67	- Unix backup: `run_before_01_backup_dotfiles.sh.tmpl` (repo `daviddwlee84/dotfiles`)
68	- chezmoi run-script ordering: https://www.chezmoi.io/reference/target-types/#scripts
69
```
</details></tool-use>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'm seeing the full picture now. The loader is a minimal implementation without any interactivity safeguards, and it sources every fragment it finds. The critical piece is understanding the OneDrive redirect—when Documents gets redirected to OneDrive, the actual `$PROFILE` path shifts to `~/OneDrive/Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`, which contains a stub that then sources the managed loader from the original Documents location.

The real concern is that if those OneDrive files are cloud-only or dehydrated, reading them triggers a network fetch to hydrate them, which can cause noticeable delays. The stub itself is small, but if the managed loader is also stored under the redirected Documents folder, that's another potential hydration hit. The setup writes to both the literal `~/Documents\PowerShell\...` path and the OneDrive-aware `$PROFILE` location, so when KFM is active, both paths end up pointing to OneDrive storage.

Looking at the actual startup costs, the expensive operations are the native subprocess spawns — each one is slow on Windows, especially on a cold start, taking 100-300ms per process. In the 10_tools fragment, there are multiple Get-Command probes followed by spawning starship, zoxide, atuin, and fzf, each of which does initialization work and potentially starts background services. So that's roughly 5-6 external process spawns from 10_tools alone, each taking 100-300ms when cold, plus the module path scanning overhead from PSFzf. In 00_env.ps1, Get-Command for nvim does a PATH lookup rather than spawning a process, but with many PATH entries and OneDrive it can still add latency. Then 40_copilot.ps1 adds another module discovery scan and imports the Copilot module, which runs its initialization code including Set-StrictMode.

Looking at the Copilot module itself, it turns out the import is relatively cheap — it's just parsing the module file and defining functions, with no network calls or spawning happening at import time. The actual network-dependent operations like Test-CopilotAlive only run when you invoke the commands, not during the initial import. That's a key finding since I was initially suspicious the module import might be a bottleneck.

Moving into 98_tv_cache.ps1, there's another Get-Command probe for tv and a directory creation call.

Enumerating aliases and functions... Line 3 checks for yazi with Get-Command, then just defines the function—also cheap at load. Now I'm tallying up all the Get-Command probes across the fragments: about 14 total spread across the env, tools, aliases, yazi, tv cache, and copilot fragments, plus 3 module discovery calls for PSFzf, Copilot, and PSReadLine.

The real bottleneck is those native process spawns—starship init, zoxide init, atuin init, direnv hook, and tv init. Five external spawns shouldn't add up to 8.8 seconds though, maybe 1-2 at most. I'm wondering if atuin init is the culprit here, possibly trying to connect to a sync daemon or checking for network updates when it initializes.

But there's another angle: on a fresh Windows machine with antivirus enabled, Windows Defender real-time scanning each newly-spawned executable (starship.exe, zoxide.exe, atuin.exe, direnv.exe, tv.exe, fd.exe) plus the overhead of resolving each Get-Command Application query could easily add hundreds of milliseconds per spawn. And Get-Command itself is expensive on Windows—it searches every directory in $PATH for matching executables with each PATHEXT extension, which gets slow if PATH is long or includes network paths like OneDrive, and those lookups aren't necessarily cached across all 14 probes.

Module discovery with Get-Module -ListAvailable scans every directory in PSModulePath, and if that includes a OneDrive-redirected Documents\PowerShell\Modules folder, it forces OneDrive to hydrate those module manifests—potentially very slow. There are three such calls happening (PSFzf, Copilot, PSReadLine). Plus the profile and modules themselves being stored on OneDrive means reading the loader stub and managed loader from a network location. When importing the Copilot module, parsing 664 lines is fine, but Import-Module also triggers Get-Module -ListAvailable style resolution against the PSModulePath that was just prepended locally.

The strongest candidate for most of that 8.8 seconds on a fresh machine is likely the combination of native tool initialization spawns, Windows Defender scanning, and OneDrive hydration during those three Get-Module -ListAvailable scans. I should present the evidence with line numbers and let the user draw their own conclusions rather than overclaiming. I've verified there's no huge tree being scanned by Get-Module -ListAvailable, and the tv cache regeneration only probes tv without running it. I also confirmed there are no network calls during load—the Copilot module has Invoke-RestMethod and Invoke-WebRequest but they're only inside functions, not at import time.

The psm1 file only has top-level statements for Set-StrictMode, function definitions, one script variable, and Export-ModuleMember—no executable calls that would spawn processes or make network requests at import. The tv cache regeneration happens in-process via aliases.txt on every startup, and tv init spawns tv at line 44 of 10_tools, so I need to check whether that init does cache generation.

Looking at the vi/vim aliases in 20_aliases.ps1, they're conditionally set to nvim only if Get-Command nvim succeeds at load time. The user has nvim installed via scoop but reports vi/vim aren't recognized—this is the core contradiction I need to resolve. The most likely explanation is that the profile never loaded due to the OneDrive redirect issue, which would prevent the alias definitions from running. The aliases are only set during profile load, so if neovim wasn't installed when the shell started, the `vi` alias wouldn't exist—which matches the "not recognized" error the user saw. They'd need to restart the shell after installing neovim for the alias to take effect. Line 30 in 00_env also guards the EDITOR variable the same way, only setting it to nvim if nvim is already present. The aliases only get created if nvim resolves at profile load time, so if it was installed after the shell started or the scoop shims weren't on PATH yet, the guard fails and no alias exists — that's why it's not recognized. A reload after installing should fix it. Let me do a few more searches across the profile directory to check for any other vi/vim alias definitions and verify there's nothing else going on with PATH setup or command resolution.

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-10 16:44:42Z)**_

I now have a complete picture. Here are my findings.

## Summary

The loader (`Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`, lines 18-23) dot-sources every `profile.d/*.ps1` unconditionally — **there is no interactive/non-interactive guard anywhere**, so all the expensive work below runs on every shell, including non-interactive ones. The dominant cost is almost certainly the **5-6 external-process init spawns in `10_tools.ps1`** (each `.exe` cold-launched + Defender-scanned on a fresh machine), compounded by **3 `Get-Module -ListAvailable` scans** of `PSModulePath` (which on a OneDrive-redirected `Documents\PowerShell\Modules` forces cloud-file hydration) and **~14 `Get-Command` PATH probes**. Notably, the Copilot module does **no** network/spawn work at import — it only defines functions — so it is *not* the bottleneck despite appearances.

---

## 1. Fragment inventory (source: `dot_config/powershell/profile.d/`)

| Prefix | File | What it does |
|---|---|---|
| 00 | `00_env.ps1` | UTF-8 console, XDG dirs, `STARSHIP_CONFIG`/`YAZI_CONFIG_HOME`/`UV_PYTHON_BIN_DIR`, sets `EDITOR=nvim`, prepends `~/.local/bin` + `~/scoop/shims` to PATH |
| 05 | `05_mirrors.ps1.tmpl` | China mirror env vars; renders only if `useChineseMirror` — pure env assignment |
| 10 | `10_tools.ps1` | **Activates starship, zoxide, atuin, fzf/PSFzf, direnv, tv** — the hot fragment |
| 20 | `20_aliases.ps1` | eza/bat/nvim/lazygit aliases + `which`/`touch`/git shortcuts/`reload`/`cas`/`cau` functions |
| 28 | `28_tldr.ps1` | Defines `tldrf` function only (no load-time work) |
| 30 | `30_apps.ps1` | Defines app/audio/clipboard helper functions only (no load-time work) |
| 35 | `35_yazi.ps1` | `Get-Command yazi` probe + defines `y` function |
| 40 | `40_copilot.ps1` | `Get-Module -ListAvailable Copilot` + `Import-Module Copilot` |
| 90 | `90_psreadline.ps1.tmpl` | `Get-Module -ListAvailable PSReadLine` + `Import-Module PSReadLine` + options |
| 98 | `98_tv_cache.ps1` | Regenerates `~/.cache/tv/aliases.txt` on **every** startup (in-process) |

---

## 2 & 3. Expensive load-time operations, ranked, with exact lines

### TIER 1 — external process spawns (likely dominant), all in `10_tools.ps1`
Each of these cold-launches a native `.exe` and captures its output at startup. On a fresh machine every one is uncached and gets Defender-scanned:

```
10_tools.ps1:6      Invoke-Expression (& starship init powershell)
10_tools.ps1:11     Invoke-Expression (& { (zoxide init powershell --cmd cd | Out-String) })
10_tools.ps1:19     Invoke-Expression (& atuin init powershell | Out-String)
10_tools.ps1:36     Invoke-Expression (& direnv hook pwsh | Out-String)
10_tools.ps1:44     $tvInit = (tv init power-shell 2>$null | Out-String)
10_tools.ps1:45     if (-not $tvInit.Trim()) { $tvInit = (tv init powershell 2>$null | Out-String) }
```
That is **5 guaranteed spawns** (starship, zoxide, atuin, direnv, tv), and **6 if** `tv init power-shell` returns empty (line 45 re-spawns `tv`). `atuin init` in particular can also touch its SQLite state / attempt daemon contact. These are the first thing I'd profile.

### TIER 2 — module discovery scans of `PSModulePath`
`Get-Module -ListAvailable` walks *every* directory on `PSModulePath` and reads manifests. If `Documents\PowerShell\Modules` is OneDrive-redirected (this repo explicitly handles KFM redirection in `run_onchange_after_02_profile_redirect.ps1`), this forces on-demand hydration of cloud-only files — potentially seconds:
```
10_tools.ps1:28     if (Get-Module -ListAvailable -Name PSFzf) {
40_copilot.ps1:4    if (Get-Module -ListAvailable -Name Copilot -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) {
90_psreadline.ps1.tmpl:3   if (Get-Module -ListAvailable -Name PSReadLine) {
```
Plus the three corresponding `Import-Module` calls (`10_tools.ps1:29`, `40_copilot.ps1:8`, `90_psreadline.ps1.tmpl:4`).

### TIER 3 — `Get-Command` PATH probes (~14 total)
Each `Get-Command <app>` for an Application searches every PATH dir × PATHEXT. Counts per file: `10_tools.ps1`=7 (starship, zoxide, atuin, fzf, fd, direnv, tv), `20_aliases.ps1`=6 (but 4 at load: eza L5, bat L12, nvim L17, lazygit L21; the other 2 are inside the `which` function), `00_env.ps1`=1 (nvim L30), `35_yazi.ps1`=1 (yazi L3), `98_tv_cache.ps1`=1 at load (tv L5; the 2nd is a probe-free `Get-Command -CommandType Function`). Individually cheap, but they add up and each is subject to PATH length + Defender.

### TIER 4 — per-startup filesystem work (`98_tv_cache.ps1`, small but unconditional)
```
98_tv_cache.ps1:7   New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $dir | Out-Null
98_tv_cache.ps1:9   Get-Alias | Sort-Object Name | ForEach-Object { ... }
98_tv_cache.ps1:10  Get-Command -CommandType Function ... (enumerates ALL functions)
98_tv_cache.ps1:13  $lines | Set-Content -Path (Join-Path $dir 'aliases.txt') -Encoding utf8
```
This regenerates the tv aliases cache on **every** shell start. It is fully in-process (does **not** spawn `tv` — only the `Get-Command tv` guard on L5), so it's minor, but it's unconditional churn with no change-detection.

### Negative findings (NOT costs — rule these out)
- **No network calls at load.** The only `http` hits are env-var *strings* in `05_mirrors.ps1.tmpl:4-10`. All `Invoke-RestMethod`/`Invoke-WebRequest` live **inside Copilot functions** (`Test-CopilotAlive`, doctor, etc.), never at import.
- **The Copilot module does no expensive work at import.** `Copilot.psm1`'s only top-level statements are `Set-StrictMode -Off` (L24), function definitions, the `$script:CopilotHereKeys` array (L446), and `Export-ModuleMember` (L662). No proxy/server start, no `bunx`, no probe runs until a `copilot-*` command is invoked. So `40_copilot.ps1` costs ~= module-discovery + parsing 664 lines, not runtime.
- **No large-tree walks / `-Recurse` / OneDrive tree reads** in any fragment. `35_yazi.ps1:5-8` (`New-TemporaryFile`, `Get-Content`) is inside the `y` function, not at load. `20_aliases.ps1:51` `New-Item` is inside `touch`.
- **No `tv` channel regeneration that spawns `tv`** at startup — only the in-process aliases.txt write in 98.

---

## 4. Existing lazy-loading / guards

- **Guards, yes; laziness, mostly no.** Every tool block is wrapped in `if (Get-Command … -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)`, so a missing tool no-ops — but when the tool *is* present the full init spawn runs eagerly every time. There is **no** caching of `starship/zoxide/atuin/direnv/tv init` output to a file (the common speed fix), **no** background-job deferral, and **no** `if (interactive)` gate in the loader.
- The only real deferral is architectural: all of `30_apps.ps1`, `28_tldr.ps1`, most of `20_aliases.ps1`, and the entire Copilot module define functions that do their heavy lifting only when *called*.
- `98_tv_cache.ps1` writes a cache but has no staleness check — it rewrites unconditionally rather than skipping when nothing changed.

---

## 5. vi / vim aliases

Yes — defined in **`dot_config/powershell/profile.d/20_aliases.ps1`**, lines 17-20, and they map to nvim:
```
17  if (Get-Command nvim -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) {
18      Set-Alias -Name vim -Value nvim -Scope Global
19      Set-Alias -Name vi  -Value nvim -Scope Global
20  }
```
They exist **only** if `Get-Command nvim` succeeds *at profile-load time*. That is the whole explanation for "`vi`/`vim` not recognized" on the fresh machine: the aliases are created conditionally, so if `nvim` wasn't resolvable when *that* shell started, no alias is created. On a fresh box the likely trigger is ordering — `nvim` (scoop) was installed *after* the measuring shell launched, or `~/scoop/shims` wasn't yet on PATH when 20 ran. Note `00_env.ps1:36-45` prepends `~/scoop/shims` only if `Test-Path` succeeds, and `00_env.ps1:30` sets `EDITOR=nvim` behind the same `Get-Command nvim` guard — so if `vi`/`vim` are missing, `$env:EDITOR` is probably unset too. Fix is a `reload` / new shell after neovim is installed; these are not persistent aliases. No other fragment defines `vi`/`vim` (the `90_psreadline` "vi" hit on line 16 is just a comment about Vi edit-mode).

**Relevant files:** `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/dot_config/powershell/profile.d/10_tools.ps1`, `.../20_aliases.ps1`, `.../40_copilot.ps1`, `.../90_psreadline.ps1.tmpl`, `.../98_tv_cache.ps1`, `.../00_env.ps1`, `.../modules/Copilot/Copilot.psm1`, and the loader `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`.

---

## 1. CI render+parse pattern (`.github/workflows/windows.yml`)

The workflow lives at the expected path: `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/.github/workflows/windows.yml`. The relevant step is **"Parse-check rendered .ps1.tmpl scripts"** (lines 100–113):

```yaml
      - name: Parse-check rendered .ps1.tmpl scripts
        shell: pwsh
        run: |
          $failed = $false
          Get-ChildItem -Recurse -Filter *.ps1.tmpl | ForEach-Object {
            $rendered = Get-Content -Raw $_.FullName | chezmoi execute-template --source .
            if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0 -or -not $rendered) { Write-Host "RENDER FAIL: $($_.Name)"; $failed = $true; return }
            $errors = $null
            [System.Management.Automation.Language.Parser]::ParseInput($rendered, [ref]$null, [ref]$errors) | Out-Null
            if ($errors) { Write-Host "PARSE FAIL: $($_.Name)"; $errors | ForEach-Object { Write-Host "  $_" }; $failed = $true }
            else { Write-Host "OK:   $($_.Name)" }
          }
          if ($failed) { throw 'A rendered template failed to render or parse' }
```

**Answer to your key question:** It iterates over **ALL** `*.ps1.tmpl` automatically via `Get-ChildItem -Recurse -Filter *.ps1.tmpl` (line 105) — there is **no explicit list**. Renaming your redirect script to `run_onchange_after_02_profile_redirect.ps1.tmpl` gets it picked up **automatically**; no manual CI edit needed. It renders each with `chezmoi execute-template --source .` (line 106) and parses with `[System.Management.Automation.Language.Parser]::ParseInput` (line 109).

Two supporting notes:
- The **Lint step** (lines 33–38) runs `Invoke-ScriptAnalyzer -Path . -Recurse` over the whole tree — but that lints the *raw* `.tmpl` text (with `{{ include }}` still in it), so PSScriptAnalyzer sees the here-string as opaque. Your embedded loader is validated by the parse step above, not this one.

## 2. Existing `{{ include }}` usage

There are exactly **two** `{{ include }}` call sites in the repo (both in the editor-overlays script), plus a related-but-different `{{ template }}` pattern for the skill body. Grep results (excluding `.specstory/` history):

```powershell
# Embedded at render time from the (chezmoi-ignored) editors/ sources — so this
# script is self-contained and re-runs when they change.

{{ include "editors/vscode-overlay.json" }}
'@
$keybindingsJson = @'
{{ include "editors/keybindings.json" }}
'@
```

Key mechanics to copy: the included content is wrapped in a **single-quoted here-string** `@' ... '@` and assigned to a PowerShell variable. Single-quoted (literal) here-string is important — the loader content contains `$HOME`, `$env:...`, backticks, and `"` that must NOT be interpreted by PowerShell at runtime. The `{{ include "..." }}` argument is a **path relative to the chezmoi source root** (here `editors/...`; for you it would be `{{ include "Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1" }}`).

This renders the shared partial `.chezmoitemplates/dotfiles-windows-skill.md` **as a template** (with `.` data context, so `{{ .role }}` etc. inside it get evaluated). This is NOT what you want for the loader — `{{ include }}` inserts the file **verbatim as text** (no template evaluation), which is correct for embedding a literal `.ps1` whose content is not a chezmoi template. Note `.chezmoitemplates/` is the special dir for named `template`/`includeTemplate` partials; `include` by contrast takes any source-relative path.

## 3. run_onchange hashing — confirmation

**Your understanding is correct.** chezmoi re-runs a `run_onchange_` script whenever its **rendered/final content** changes (it stores a hash of the script contents after template rendering). Embedding the loader via `{{ include }}` therefore folds the loader's bytes into the rendered script, so editing the loader changes the hash and re-fires the script.

Repo statements supporting this:

- The editor-overlays script comment states exactly this behavior for the include pattern. `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_20_editor_overlays.ps1.tmpl`, lines 8–9:
  ```

  # script is self-contained and re-runs when they change.
  ```
  And line 2–3: `# ... Re-runs when either embedded source file changes.`

- The backup backlog note states the content-hash re-run rule explicitly (for `run_once_before_`, same hashing mechanism). `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/backlog/align-windows-backup-with-unix-run-before-unified-dir.md`, lines 23–26:
  ```
   - Windows: `.chezmoiscripts/run_once_before_01_backup.ps1.tmpl` —
     `run_once_before_` fires **only on the first apply** (or when the script
     content hash changes). After the initial snapshot, editing a local file
     and re-applying does **not** back it up. This is the real gap.
  ```

- The chezmoi run-script ordering reference is linked at `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/backlog/align-windows-backup-with-unix-run-before-unified-dir.md` line 68: `https://www.chezmoi.io/reference/target-types/#scripts`.

Note: the current plain `.ps1` redirect script has NO way to observe loader changes — its own text never changes when the loader changes, so it only re-fires if you edit the script itself. Your conversion fixes exactly that gap (this is also captured in the prior planning transcript at `.specstory/history/2026-07-10_15-43-21Z-ps1-irm-https-raw.md:690`, though that's your own planning note, not repo doc).

## 4. Backup conventions

Two distinct backup styles coexist. Your plan should stay consistent with whichever the redirect script already uses (the fixed-filename style).

**a) Timestamped-subdir style — the first-apply snapshot.** `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/.chezmoiscripts/run_once_before_01_backup.ps1.tmpl`, lines 18–27:

```powershell
$existing = $targets | Where-Object { Test-Path $_ }
if ($existing) {
    $stamp = Get-Date -Format 'yyyyMMdd-HHmmss'
    $dir = Join-Path $HOME ".dotfiles-backup/$stamp"
    New-Item -ItemType Directory -Force -Path $dir | Out-Null
    foreach ($t in $existing) {
        Copy-Item -Recurse -Force -LiteralPath $t -Destination (Join-Path $dir (Split-Path $t -Leaf))
    }
    Write-Host "backed up $($existing.Count) item(s) -> $dir"
}
```

So the timestamp format is `yyyyMMdd-HHmmss` (line 20), e.g. `~/.dotfiles-backup/20260711-000448/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`. This runs once and captures the **literal** `~/Documents/PowerShell/...` path (line 11 of that file lists it as target #1).

**b) Fixed-filename style — the OneDrive-redirected profile.** The redirect script uses a **stable, non-timestamped** filename with a `.onedrive.bak` suffix, guarded so it only writes once (never overwrites an existing backup). `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/.chezmoiscripts/run_onchange_after_02_profile_redirect.ps1`, lines 37–43:

```powershell
                $backupDir = Join-Path $HOME '.dotfiles-backup'

                if (-not (Test-Path -LiteralPath $bak)) {
                    Copy-Item -LiteralPath $realFull -Destination $bak -Force
                    Write-Host "==> backed up existing profile -> $bak" -ForegroundColor Cyan
                }
```

The rationale for this second backup is in the script's own comment (lines 30–32): the `run_once_before` snapshot only guards the literal `~/Documents` path, not the OneDrive-redirected `$PROFILE` — so the redirect script backs that up itself.

**Common convention across both:** backup root is `~/.dotfiles-backup` (hyphen) — lines 37/21 respectively. The naming inconsistency vs the Unix repo's `~/.dotfiles_backup` (underscore) is logged at `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/backlog/align-windows-backup-with-unix-run-before-unified-dir.md` lines 37–39. For your converted script, keeping the existing fixed-filename `Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1.onedrive.bak` under `~/.dotfiles-backup` (unchanged) is the consistent choice.

                $bak = Join-Path $backupDir 'Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1.onedrive.bak'
                if (-not (Test-Path -LiteralPath $bak)) {
                    Copy-Item -LiteralPath $realFull -Destination $bak -Force
                    Write-Host "==&gt; backed up existing profile -&gt; $bak" -ForegroundColor Cyan
                }
```

The rationale for this second backup is in the script's own comment (lines 30–32): the `run_once_before` snapshot only guards the literal `~/Documents` path, not the OneDrive-redirected `$PROFILE` — so the redirect script backs that up itself.

**Common convention across both:** backup root is `~/.dotfiles-backup` (hyphen) — lines 37/21 respectively. The naming inconsistency vs the Unix repo's `~/.dotfiles_backup` (underscore) is logged at `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/backlog/align-windows-backup-with-unix-run-before-unified-dir.md` lines 37–39. For your converted script, keeping the existing fixed-filename `Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1.onedrive.bak` under `~/.dotfiles-backup` (unchanged) is the consistent choice.

## 5. How `Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1` is mapped

**Confirmed:** the source path is **literally** `Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1` — no `dot_`, no `private_`, no `.tmpl`, no encryption. Verified two ways:
- `find Documents -type f` → `Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1` (only file).
- `ls -la Documents/PowerShell/` shows plain `Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1` (1.2k, no attribute prefix).

Because `Documents` is a normal (non-`dot_`) directory name, chezmoi maps it verbatim to the target `~/Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`. **It is verbatim, NOT a template** (no `.tmpl` extension) — the file content is exactly what lands on disk. Full content is at `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1` (28 lines; a thin loader that dot-sources `~/.config/powershell/profile.d/*.ps1` then `local.ps1`).

**`.chezmoiroot`:** does **not exist** (`no .chezmoiroot`), so the source root is the repo root — `Documents/...` targets `~/Documents/...` directly.

**`.chezmoiignore`:** `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/.chezmoiignore` does **not** list `Documents/` or the profile path, so the profile **is** deployed to `~/Documents/PowerShell/...`. (The ignore file excludes repo-meta like `docs/**`, `editors/**`, `scripts/**`, `tests/**`, `packages/**`, `.github/**`, etc., but not `Documents`.)

Corroborating docs (all describe the same target path):
- `README.md:50` and `docs/index.md:42`: `` | `Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1` | `$PROFILE` loader. | ``
- `AGENTS.md:51`: "Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1 is a thin $PROFILE loader that dot-sources dot_config/powershell/profile.d/*.ps1 in numeric order..."

**Implication for your `{{ include }}`:** the include path argument must be the **source-relative** path `Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1` (matching the actual source layout, since there's no `.chezmoiroot`). Because the file is verbatim (not itself a template), `include` inserts its exact bytes — good. Also note the redirect script's `$managed` variable (line 19) uses backslashes `Documents\PowerShell\...` while the backup script (line 11) and include path use forward slashes; both resolve on Windows, but keep the include argument forward-slashed to match chezmoi convention.

## 6. Existing tests touching the loader / redirect script

**None directly.** The only Pester file is `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/tests/Copilot.Tests.ps1` (`find . -name '*.Tests.ps1'` returns just this one). It tests the **Copilot module only** — package-flavor detection, model resolution/normalization, served-model parsing, effective-model precedence, and the exported command surface. It does **not** touch the `$PROFILE` loader, the `profile.d` fragments, or the redirect script. Representative assertions:

CI runs Pester generically — `/Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-windows-dotfiles/.github/workflows/windows.yml` lines 115–119 do `if (Get-ChildItem -Recurse -Filter *.Tests.ps1) { Invoke-Pester -CI -Path . -Output Detailed }`. So if you add a `*.Tests.ps1` covering the redirect logic it would be auto-discovered, but **there is no existing test to update** for this change.

### Bottom line for your conversion

- Rename to `run_onchange_after_02_profile_redirect.ps1.tmpl`; CI parse-check picks it up automatically (Q1).
- Embed the loader with a single-quoted here-string: `$loader = @'` / `{{ include "Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1" }}` / `'@`, exactly mirroring the editor-overlays pattern (Q2). Single-quoted is essential because the loader contains `$HOME`/`$env:` that must stay literal.
- The include folds the loader bytes into the rendered script, so `run_onchange` re-fires when you edit the loader — which is the whole point (Q3).
- Keep the existing fixed-filename backup `~/.dotfiles-backup/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1.onedrive.bak` (Q4).
- Loader source path is verbatim `Documents/PowerShell/Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1`, not ignored, no `.chezmoiroot` — so the include path is exactly that (Q5).
- No existing tests to update (Q6).

One thing to double-check when you implement: the loader's line 47–50 stub in the current redirect script embeds `$managedFull` — that's runtime pwsh logic and unrelated to the include; the include only needs to add the loader-content constant if your new design writes the loader body somewhere. If the redirect script's job stays "write a one-line dot-source stub," embedding the full loader content is only needed to trigger re-hashing — you may end up embedding it into a discarded/verification variable purely so the hash tracks loader edits (the editor-overlays script actually *uses* its embedded content; make sure yours either uses it or clearly comments why it's embedded).</result>

## 1. `uv python install --default` failure

**Location: lines 186–194.**

```
186	# Default Python, uv-managed. --default also creates unversioned python/python3
187	# (not just python3.x) in UV_PYTHON_BIN_DIR (~/.local/bin, on PATH via 00_env
188	# ahead of the Store's python.exe app-alias). Needs uv >= 0.8.
189	if (Have uv) {
190	    Info 'uv python install --default'
191	    $env:UV_PYTHON_BIN_DIR = Join-Path $HOME '.local\bin'
192	    uv python install --default 2>&1 | Out-Host
193	    if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Register-Failure 'uv:python' }
194	}
```

- **Gating:** the whole block is guarded by `if (Have uv)` (line 189), i.e. it only runs if `uv` is on PATH.
- **Failure registration:** line 193 — when `$LASTEXITCODE -ne 0`, it calls `Register-Failure 'uv:python'`, which is exactly the `WARNING: FAILED (skipped): uv:python` line in your log.
- **Root-cause note:** the comment on line 188 says **"Needs uv >= 0.8"** — that assumption is now stale. `uv` is installed unpinned by scoop (line 142, plain `'uv'`), so a fresh machine gets latest (your 0.11.28). In current uv, `--default` was moved behind preview mode, so the bare `uv python install --default` now errors with the `--preview`-required message you saw and trips `Register-Failure`. The fix surface is line 192 (add `--preview`) and the outdated comment on line 188.
- **Docs also carry the stale command** (not the script, but relevant if you change the flag): `docs/tools.md:38`, `docs/rationale.md:55`, `docs/tools.zh-TW.md:37`, `docs/rationale.zh-TW.md:47-48` all document `uv python install --default` without `--preview`.

**Location: lines 169–181** (preceded by an explanatory comment block at lines 162–168).

```
169	if (Have gh) {
170	    gh auth status *> $null
171	    if ($LASTEXITCODE -eq 0) {

174	            Info 'gh extension install dlvhdr/gh-dash'

176	            if ($LASTEXITCODE -ne 0) { Register-Failure 'gh-ext:gh-dash' }
177	        }
178	    } else {
179	        Info 'gh not authenticated -- skipping gh-dash (gh auth login, then reapply)'
180	    }
181	}
```

- The exact log line comes from **line 179**. It is emitted via `Info` (a `Write-Host "==> ..."`, defined line 38), which is why it appears as `==> gh not authenticated -- skipping gh-dash (gh auth login, then reapply)`.
- Key detail: this "skip" path does **not** call `Register-Failure` — an unauthenticated fresh machine is treated as an expected no-op, not a failure. It only registers `gh-ext:gh-dash` (line 176) if gh *is* authenticated and the extension install actually fails. So gh-dash being skipped will **not** show up in the final failure summary.

## 4. Failure collection, error-preference setup, and exit behavior

**Setup — lines 23–25:**
```
23	$ErrorActionPreference = 'Continue'
24	if (Test-Path variable:PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference) { $PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference = $false }
25	$script:Failures = [System.Collections.Generic.List[string]]::new()
```
- Line 23 forces `Continue` so a terminating-ish error doesn't abort the run.
- Line 24 defensively sets `$PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference = $false` (guarded by a `Test-Path` because the variable only exists on newer pwsh) so native-command non-zero exits don't throw.

**`Register-Failure` — line 40:**
```
40	function Register-Failure($what) { $script:Failures.Add($what); Write-Warning "FAILED (skipped): $what" }
```
Every subsystem funnels here: `scoop:<name>` (line 91), `winget:<id>` (lines 97, 108), `msstore:<id>` (lines 115, 121), `npm:<pkg>` (lines 126, 130), `gh-ext:gh-dash` (176), `uv:python` (193), `uv:litellm` (310), `az-ext:azure-devops` (327).

**Summary block — lines 331–337 (end of file):**
```
331	if ($script:Failures.Count -gt 0) {
332	    Write-Warning ("package install: {0} item(s) failed and were skipped:" -f $script:Failures.Count)
333	    $script:Failures | ForEach-Object { Write-Warning "  - $_" }
334	    Write-Warning "Re-run 'just apply' (or 'chezmoi apply') to retry, or install them manually."
335	} else {
336	    Info 'package install complete — no failures'
337	}
```

**Exit behavior — flag this:** there is **no explicit `exit 0` (or any `exit`) statement anywhere in the script.** I grepped the whole file: the only match for `exit` is a comment on line 302 (`exit 1625`). The script relies on falling off the end after the summary block, whose last statements are cmdlets (`Write-Warning` / `Info`→`Write-Host`), which do not modify `$LASTEXITCODE`.

- The interpreter is configured in `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl:32-35` as `pwsh` with `args = ["-NoProfile", "-NonInteractive"]` — no `-Command`/explicit exit-code wrapper.
- Because `$ErrorActionPreference = 'Continue'` and there's no `exit N`, the intent is clearly "always succeed / never abort chezmoi apply." However, since the exit code is left implicit, a stale non-zero `$LASTEXITCODE` from the last native command executed (e.g. a failing `winget`/`az`/`uv` invocation late in the run) could in principle propagate as the process exit code under some pwsh invocation semantics. If the "must exit 0" invariant is load-bearing, adding an explicit `exit 0` after line 337 would make it guaranteed rather than incidental. I did not find the invariant written down in `docs/` — it appears to be an unenforced convention rather than a documented/tested contract.

- **§1 uv:** real bug — the command needs `--preview` on modern uv; comment on line 188 ("uv >= 0.8") is the stale assumption. Same stale command echoed in 4 docs files.

- **§4 exit:** no explicit `exit 0`; the "must exit 0" invariant is implied by design (`Continue` + collect-and-report) but not enforced in code or documented.

It only registers `gh-ext:gh-dash` (line 176) if gh *is* authenticated and the extension install actually fails. So gh-dash being skipped will **not** show up in the final failure summary.

## 4. Failure collection, error-preference setup, and exit behavior

**Setup — lines 23–25:**
```
23	$ErrorActionPreference = 'Continue'
24	if (Test-Path variable:PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference) { $PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference = $false }
25	$script:Failures = [System.Collections.Generic.List[string]]::new()
```
- Line 23 forces `Continue` so a terminating-ish error doesn't abort the run.
- Line 24 defensively sets `$PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference = $false` (guarded by a `Test-Path` because the variable only exists on newer pwsh) so native-command non-zero exits don't throw.

**`Register-Failure` — line 40:**
```
40	function Register-Failure($what) { $script:Failures.Add($what); Write-Warning "FAILED (skipped): $what" }
```
Every subsystem funnels here: `scoop:&lt;name&gt;` (line 91), `winget:&lt;id&gt;` (lines 97, 108), `msstore:&lt;id&gt;` (lines 115, 121), `npm:&lt;pkg&gt;` (lines 126, 130), `gh-ext:gh-dash` (176), `uv:python` (193), `uv:litellm` (310), `az-ext:azure-devops` (327).

**Summary block — lines 331–337 (end of file):**
```
331	if ($script:Failures.Count -gt 0) {
332	    Write-Warning ("package install: {0} item(s) failed and were skipped:" -f $script:Failures.Count)
333	    $script:Failures | ForEach-Object { Write-Warning "  - $_" }
334	    Write-Warning "Re-run 'just apply' (or 'chezmoi apply') to retry, or install them manually."
335	} else {
336	    Info 'package install complete — no failures'
337	}
```

**Exit behavior — flag this:** there is **no explicit `exit 0` (or any `exit`) statement anywhere in the script.** I grepped the whole file: the only match for `exit` is a comment on line 302 (`exit 1625`). The script relies on falling off the end after the summary block, whose last statements are cmdlets (`Write-Warning` / `Info`→`Write-Host`), which do not modify `$LASTEXITCODE`.

- The interpreter is configured in `.chezmoi.toml.tmpl:32-35` as `pwsh` with `args = ["-NoProfile", "-NonInteractive"]` — no `-Command`/explicit exit-code wrapper.
- Because `$ErrorActionPreference = 'Continue'` and there's no `exit N`, the intent is clearly "always succeed / never abort chezmoi apply." However, since the exit code is left implicit, a stale non-zero `$LASTEXITCODE` from the last native command executed (e.g. a failing `winget`/`az`/`uv` invocation late in the run) could in principle propagate as the process exit code under some pwsh invocation semantics. If the "must exit 0" invariant is load-bearing, adding an explicit `exit 0` after line 337 would make it guaranteed rather than incidental. I did not find the invariant written down in `docs/` — it appears to be an unenforced convention rather than a documented/tested contract.

4	# localized output, …) render as mojibake on legacy-codepage systems (e.g. GBK /
5	# cp936 on zh-* Windows). Guarded so a redirected/headless host can't error the

12	
13	# XDG-style base dirs. Several tools honor these on Windows (starship, atuin,
14	# zoxide state, our own copilot module) so we set them for a tidy $HOME.
15	if (-not $env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME) { $env:XDG_CONFIG_HOME = Join-Path $HOME '.config' }
16	if (-not $env:XDG_DATA_HOME)   { $env:XDG_DATA_HOME   = Join-Path $HOME '.local/share' }
17	if (-not $env:XDG_STATE_HOME)  { $env:XDG_STATE_HOME  = Join-Path $HOME '.local/state' }
18	if (-not $env:XDG_CACHE_HOME)  { $env:XDG_CACHE_HOME  = Join-Path $HOME '.cache' }

23	
24	# uv-managed Python: `uv python install --default` drops python/python3 here.
25	# ~/.local/bin is prepended to PATH below, so they win over the Windows Store
26	# `python.exe` app-execution-alias.
27	$env:UV_PYTHON_BIN_DIR = Join-Path $HOME '.local/bin'

32	# Prepend user bin dirs to PATH, idempotently. scoop already adds its shims to
33	# the persisted user PATH, but a fresh session inside chezmoi apply may not have

35	# entry to backslashes (mixed slashes can fail Windows command resolution).
36	$UserPaths = @(
